ministry_positions.notes: Ordained Dec 5, 1753; settled there 24 years until death by smallpox

primary_residence.notes: Minister here 24 years 1753-1777. Yale 1751, ordained Dec 5, 1753.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Jonathan Edwards' Northampton pulpit, died of smallpox
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained in 1753, he became pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts — the church where Jonathan Edwards had preached until his dismissal in 1750. He held that pulpit for nearly 25 years, dying of smallpox on February 6, 1777, in his 48th year. His was the most prominent pastoral appointment held by any Hooker descendant of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's father, Colonel Worthington, named Worthington Parish
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Worthington, daughter of Colonel Worthington of Springfield, Massachusetts — the man who gave his name to Worthington Parish in Berlin, Connecticut, the parish where the Hart and Hooker families had long been prominent. The marriage connected the Hooker line to one of western Massachusetts's most distinguished families.

narrative_blocks.header: Smallpox took the Northampton pastor at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He served as pastor of the Northampton MA Congregational Church from 1753 until smallpox killed him in February 1777 at forty-seven. His daughter Sarah Hooker married the Northampton lawyer Caleb Strong nine months later — passing the Hooker name into the family of a future Massachusetts Governor and U.S. Senator.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards's daughter refused his pulpit and her chair sat in the vestibule.

H00525.prominent_name_reason: Wife of Gov. Caleb Strong — 6th and 10th Governor of Massachusetts (1800-1807, 1812-1816), U.S. Senator, Founding Father, delegate to the Philadelphia Convention of 1787. Daughter of Rev. John Hooker of Northampton (H00264). 5th-generation descendant of Thomas Hooker.
H00525.gravestone_inscription: In Memory of Mrs. Sarah Strong, the wife of Caleb Strong and daughter of the Rev John Hooker who having early devoted herself to the service of her Redeemer and through life exemplified the virtues & graces of his religion gently breathed out her spirit in humble reliance upon his power and love on the 12th day of February AD 1817 in the 60th year of her age.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died of smallpox before her wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Rev. John Hooker pastor of Northampton MA died of smallpox in February 1777 when she was 19. Caleb Strong, a lawyer who had joined her father's congregation five years earlier, married her in November 1777 — nine months after the funeral.

narrative_blocks.header: Matriarch of the Strong-Hooker-Dwight branch
narrative_blocks.body: Mother of nine children with Gov. Caleb Strong including Clarissa Strong Dwight, who married Timothy Dwight Jr. in 1809 and produced the New Haven Dwight-Lyman cluster — three sons of whom were Yale-educated and one of whom Sam's tree records as ancestor of two generations of Yale presidents.

political_offices.title: U.S. Senator (Class 2), Massachusetts
political_offices.notes: Member of the inaugural U.S. Senate

political_offices.title: Massachusetts Constitutional Convention
political_offices.notes: Member of committee that drafted the Massachusetts State Constitution

X00117.prominent_name_reason: Gov. Caleb Strong — Founding Father; delegate to 1787 Philadelphia Convention; helped draft Massachusetts State Constitution (1779); U.S. Senator in inaugural Senate; 6th and 10th Governor of Massachusetts; instrumental in drafting Judiciary Act of 1789 and 11th Amendment; worked on Quock Walker cases establishing slavery incompatible with Massachusetts constitution. Married Sarah Hooker (H00525), daughter of Rev. John Hooker of Northampton.

notable.notable_blurb: Governor of Massachusetts 1800-1807 (Federalist) and U.S. Senator

education.notes: Temporarily blinded by smallpox shortly after graduation — prevented him from military service during the Revolution

narrative_blocks.header: Married his pastor's daughter, 1777
narrative_blocks.body: He joined Rev. John Hooker's Northampton congregation in 1772 at age 27. Five years later, when Rev. Hooker died of smallpox in February 1777, the newly-licensed lawyer married his late pastor's daughter Sarah Hooker in November 1777. The Hooker name thus passed directly into the family of a future Founding Father.

narrative_blocks.header: U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, 1789-1796
narrative_blocks.body: In 1788 he was elected as one of the first two U.S. Senators from Massachusetts and served 1789-1796 in the Federalist majority of the early Senate. He had previously served at the 1787 Constitutional Convention as a Massachusetts delegate, though he left Philadelphia before the document was signed because of family illness.

narrative_blocks.header: Federalist Governor of Massachusetts, 1800-1807
narrative_blocks.body: He served seven consecutive one-year terms as Federalist Governor of Massachusetts 1800-1807, then a four-term return 1812-1816. During the War of 1812 his Federalist stance against the war and against New England troop mobilization drew Madison administration ire but was popular in Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of nine, source of the Strong-Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: With his wife Sarah Hooker Strong he had nine children: 5 sons and 4 daughters including Clarissa Strong Dwight, the matriarch of the New Haven Dwight-Lyman branch. The family thereby connected the Massachusetts political elite to the Hooker founding line of Hartford CT.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College class of 1797. Subsequent career as lawyer at Coal Grove, Ohio.

narrative_blocks.header: First of the Strong-Hooker line west
narrative_blocks.body: When Theodore Strong moved his family from Northampton to Coal Grove, Ohio, he carried the Hooker-Strong inheritance west of the Appalachians for the first time. His father Governor Caleb Strong had governed Massachusetts from Northampton; Theodore died at the Ohio frontier in 1855.

narrative_blocks.header: Fell into boiling water at age two, 1783
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Strong was born February 19, 1781 and died October 26, 1783 at two and a half — the Hooker genealogy records she was 'scalded by falling into a tub of boiling water.' She was the second child of Governor Caleb Strong and Sarah Hooker Strong.

HD0371.prominent_name_reason: Through her marriage to Timothy Dwight Jr., Clarissa Strong connected Thomas Hooker's line to the Edwards-Dwight family. Her father-in-law Rev. Timothy Dwight Sr. was 8th President of Yale (1795-1817); his mother Mary Edwards was daughter of Jonathan Edwards; Jonathan Edwards's wife Sarah Pierpont descended from Thomas Hooker through Mary Hooker Pierpont → James Pierpont → Sarah Pierpont Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Massachusetts governor's daughter married a hardware merchant
narrative_blocks.body: In May 1809 at age 25 she married Timothy Dwight Jr. — hardware merchant, son of 8th Yale President 'Pope' Dwight — at her father's Northampton home. Her father had just completed seven Federalist terms as MA Governor; the marriage tied the Strong political dynasty to the Dwight intellectual one.

narrative_blocks.header: Double Hooker descent for all seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Timothy Dwight Jr. was himself a Hooker descendant through his mother Mary Edwards Dwight, daughter of Esther Edwards. With her own Hooker descent through her mother Sarah Hooker, all seven of their children carried two independent lines of Thomas Hooker ancestry.

narrative_blocks.header: Her family’s papers fill a Yale archive
narrative_blocks.body: The Dwight family papers at Yale preserve the correspondence, sermons, and photographs of the clan she married into — Edwards, Hooker, Lyman, Strong, Woodbridge, and Woolsey relations all represented. She rests at Grove Street, steps from the college her father-in-law led.

institutions.institution_blurb: Harvard College class of 1803. Practiced law in Northampton Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Northampton; never left; buried there
narrative_blocks.body: Lewis Strong was born in Northampton in 1785, graduated Harvard in 1803, and returned to practice law — never leaving the town where his father the Governor had built his career. He died there October 25, 1863.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of his ten children died young
narrative_blocks.body: Lewis and Mariah Chester Strong raised ten children in Northampton, and three died in childhood: Elizabeth Chester at two, Julia Mitchell at two, Maria Chester at eight. A fourth son Theodore died at twenty-two the same month he graduated Williams College.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Rev. Phoenix in 1822 — his third wife
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Strong married Rev. Alexander Phoenix on October 28, 1822 at thirty-five — and she was his third wife, a detail EH records without comment. They lived successively at Chicopee, Massachusetts, New Haven, Connecticut, and Harlem, New York, before she died at Paterson, New Jersey in 1856.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Sarah after the sister who died
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Strong (born July 22, 1787) was named after her older sister Sarah who had died by scalding in a tub of boiling water at age two in 1783. She died June 10, 1856 at Paterson, New Jersey, outliving her son Daniel Sidney Phoenix — a Yale student who died at sixteen in 1841 — by fifteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard graduate 1810; died at twenty-two
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Strong graduated from Harvard College in 1810, following his father Caleb Strong who had graduated Harvard in 1764 — the same institution across two generations. He died May 6, 1813 at about twenty-two, just three years after graduation.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-five; mother died the year before
narrative_blocks.body: Julia A. Strong was born in 1793 and died October 1, 1818 at about twenty-five. Her mother Sarah Hooker Strong had died February 12, 1817 — Julia outlived her mother by just eighteen months.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at four in Northampton, 1799
narrative_blocks.body: Phebe Strong was born March 22, 1795 and died September 10, 1799 at four years old. She was the eighth child of Governor Caleb Strong and Sarah Hooker Strong; both parents outlived her by more than fifteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in infancy; the last of nine children
narrative_blocks.body: Philip Strong was born February 27, 1799 and died August 17, 1800 — barely six months, the ninth and final child of Governor Caleb Strong and Sarah Hooker Strong. Of the nine children, four died before adulthood.

classification.lineage_note: H00526 Seth (gen 5) → H00264 Rev. John (gen 4) → H00098 John No.2 (gen 3) → H00030 John (gen 2) → H00007 Samuel (gen 1) → H00001 Thomas

compiler_connection.notes: On the compiler's paternal (Hooker surname) line. Marriage to Abigail Gay explains the middle name 'Gay' in the compiler's father Michael Gay Hooker. The Gay family farm at Hinsdale NH became the Hooker place.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: Seth(H00526) → [descendants to trace] → Michael Gay Hooker → Samuel Talcott Hooker

open_questions.question: Father Rev. John Hooker of Northampton — EH entry number
open_questions.notes: Son of Rev. John and Sarah (Worthington) Hooker of Northampton, MA. Rev. John descends through H00098 (John No. 2, b. 1695). Need EH entry number.
open_questions.action: Look up Rev. John Hooker of Northampton in EH index

military_service.notes: EH: 'though very young he had some service' — born 1759, so approximately age 15-18 during service

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Came to the Hinsdale homestead in 1789 through his marriage to Abigail Gay; worked the farm and made hats there for fifty-five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage carried Hookers north to Hinsdale, 1789
narrative_blocks.body: Born and raised at Northampton, he served briefly in the Revolution as a teenager and learned hatmaking. His 1789 marriage to Abigail Gay of Hinsdale brought him onto her father Bunker's homestead, establishing the Hooker family on what became their New Hampshire seat for four generations.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Born and raised on her father Rev. Bunker Gay's homestead; her 1789 marriage to Seth Hooker brought him onto the farm.
Inherited the Hinsdale homestead from his father Seth; died in the house where he had been born ninety-three years earlier.

career.notes: Learned the trade in early life; pursued carriage making while also farming the homestead.
Worked the homestead through old age; gave full attention to farming after abandoning the carriage trade.

narrative_blocks.header: His sons launched three California empires
narrative_blocks.body: Six of his eleven children left Hinsdale for the West. Charles Gay built the San Francisco hardware fortune, Henry Clay founded Arizona's first cattle ranch, and John Daggett funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson.

narrative_blocks.header: Ninety-three years in one Hinsdale farmhouse
narrative_blocks.body: Born July 1791 in the room overlooking the Gay homestead and dying in March 1885, he passed his entire life on one farm in Hinsdale. The Gazetteer of Cheshire County called him 'a hale and hearty old man.'

narrative_blocks.header: Crossed to California twice in his eighties
narrative_blocks.body: After eighty he made two trips out to visit his California sons, riding the railroads across a continent he had been born too early to see imagined. He returned each time to the Hinsdale homestead.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up on the Gay-Hooker homestead before her 1824 marriage to Elihu Stebbins Jr.

narrative_blocks.header: Organized Hinsdale's first Sunday school
narrative_blocks.body: After her 1824 marriage to Elihu Stebbins Jr., she organized the first Sunday school in Hinsdale and appointed Lewis Taylor as its first superintendent. The Stebbins family had been Connecticut River Valley settlers since the 1650s.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Born and raised on the Gay-Hooker homestead before her 1820 marriage to William Hollis Taylor took her to a Taylor farm in Hinsdale.

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; Richest man in old Hampshire County

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1782, Springfield lawyer and judge
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale College in 1782 and settled at Springfield, MA, where he practiced law and rose to Judge of the Court of Common Pleas and then Judge of Probate at his death. He was a Deacon of the First Congregational Church of Springfield throughout this period.

narrative_blocks.header: Member of American Board for Foreign Missions
narrative_blocks.body: He was an active member of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM), the first US foreign-missions organization, founded 1810 in Williamstown MA. The Board sent New England missionaries to India, the Sandwich Islands (Hawaii), and Native American tribes — a defining institution of New England Federalist evangelical culture.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Her father built this house, later moved to Historic Deerfield.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, beside her husband Judge John Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Born to Springfield's mercantile gentry, 1764
narrative_blocks.body: She was born 13 December 1764 to Col. Josiah Dwight — Yale 1736, owner of an iron foundry, a potash manufactory, and 5,000 acres of Hampshire County land — and to Elizabeth Buckminster of Brookfield. Her father died in 1768 leaving an estate worth £9,458 when she was four; her mother survived to 1798.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Judge John Hooker, December 1790
narrative_blocks.body: On 18 December 1790 in Springfield she married John Hooker, Yale 1782 graduate and son of the Rev. John of Northampton. She bore him ten children between 1791 and 1808, including Dr. Worthington Hooker (H01407) and Elizabeth Dwight Hooker Packard (H01403).

education.notes: Son of John Hooker (H00527, Yale 1782). Lawyer, Springfield MA.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, Hampden County MA — with wife Rachel Breck and his parents Judge John (H00527) and Sarah Dwight Hooker

education.notes: Studied medicine at the Medical Institution of Yale College, receiving M.D. in 1817. Practiced afterward with Dr. Eli Ives in New Haven.

career.notes: Practiced with Dr. Eli Ives — Yale's Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine, the same chair later occupied (1852-1867) by George's younger brother Worthington Hooker (H01407).
Removed to Syracuse, NY where he practiced medicine for several years.
Returned east to Longmeadow MA where he continued medical practice until his death there 14 March 1884 at age 91.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1817 M.D. under Dr. Eli Ives
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale College 1814 he took his medical degree from the Medical Institution of Yale College in 1817, then practiced alongside Dr. Eli Ives in New Haven. Ives — Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine at Yale until 1852 — would later be succeeded in that chair by George's younger brother Worthington Hooker (H01407).

narrative_blocks.header: Syracuse practice, then Longmeadow homecoming
narrative_blocks.body: After his New Haven training he removed to Syracuse, New York, where he practiced medicine for some years before returning east to Longmeadow, Massachusetts. He continued practice at Longmeadow until his death there 14 March 1884 at age 91.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived ninety-one years to 1884
narrative_blocks.body: He was born 7 March 1793 to Judge John Hooker (H00527) and Sarah Dwight Hooker (X00227) at Springfield, and lived past his ninety-first birthday — dying just seven days after it on 14 March 1884 at Longmeadow. He had outlived his celebrated younger brother Dr. Worthington Hooker (H01407) by seventeen years.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousin Almira was Dr. Enoch Hale's first wife

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin Almira's widower, 1822
narrative_blocks.body: In May 1822, Sarah Hooker married Dr. Enoch Hale — the Westhampton physician who had been widowed six years earlier by her first cousin Almira Hooker. The two women shared the same Thomas Hooker lineage: Sarah through her father Judge John Hooker of Springfield, Almira through her father Dr. William Hooker of Westhampton. Sarah died at Westhampton three years later on April 25, 1825.

burial.plot_notes: Returned to Springfield Cemetery, beside her parents Judge John and Sarah Dwight Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Married Frederic Packard in Springfield, 1822
narrative_blocks.body: On 15 May 1822 she married Frederic Adolphus Packard, a Harvard 1814 graduate then editing the Hampden Federalist in Springfield. The couple moved to Philadelphia in 1829 when he became editor of American Sunday School Union publications, ultimately producing 2,000 titles including the first child's newspaper in the United States.

narrative_blocks.header: Bore five Packard children; two died young
narrative_blocks.body: Five children followed between 1827 and 1836: John Hooker Packard 1st (H02742, died on his second birthday); Frederick Jr. (H02743, lawyer Appleton WI); Mary Hooker (H02744, married Samuel Clarke Perkins of Skull and Bones); Dr. John Hooker Packard (H02745, the eminent Philadelphia surgeon); and Lewis Richard (H02746, Yale Hillhouse Professor of Greek). The eldest two were buried back in Springfield with the Hooker grandparents.

narrative_blocks.header: Died Philadelphia 1862, returned to Springfield
narrative_blocks.body: She died 5 July 1862 in Philadelphia at age 64, where she had lived since 1829. Her body was returned to Springfield Cemetery to lie beside her father Judge John and her Hooker grandparents — five years before her husband Frederic would join them in 1867.

cross_connections.display_label: Prof. Worthington Hooker married Henrietta Whitney Edwards, great-granddaughter of Jonathan.
DOUBLE HOOKER DESCENT: His second wife Henrietta Whitney Edwards was.
DOUBLE HOOKER DESCENT: His second wife Henrietta Whitney Edwards was.
Both buried Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven — Hooker descendants
was a later Yale physician who pioneered cardiac defibrillation

notable.notable_blurb: physician, Yale Professor of Theory and Practice of

narrative_blocks.header: Physician and Patient — landmark US medical ethics
narrative_blocks.body: His 1849 book Physician and Patient has been described as the most original United States contribution to medical ethics in the nineteenth century. It argued for honesty with patients, condemned medical deception even when well-intentioned, and shaped American physician-patient communication norms for a generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine
narrative_blocks.body: After twenty-three years practicing medicine in Norwich, Connecticut, he was chosen in 1852 to succeed Dr. Eli Ives as Yale's Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine. He held the chair until his death, combining teaching with active medical practice and continued publication.

narrative_blocks.header: Vice President of American Medical Association, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: He was elected Vice President of the American Medical Association in 1864 — among the highest national positions in mid-19th-century American medicine. The AMA at that time was the leading professional voice for orthodox medicine against homeopathy, eclectic medicine, and the prison sanitary movement.

narrative_blocks.header: Worthington Hooker School named for him
narrative_blocks.body: The Worthington Hooker School in New Haven's East Rock neighborhood still bears his name and is part of the city's public school system. The school was named in recognition of his Yale professorship and his presence in New Haven's intellectual establishment.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker branches
narrative_blocks.body: His second marriage on 31 January 1855 to Henrietta Whitney Edwards — daughter of Connecticut Governor Henry W. Edwards — also reunited two branches of the Hooker family. Henrietta descended from Mary Hooker (1673-1740) through her great-great-grandmother Sarah Pierpont and her great-great-grandfather Rev. James Pierpont, making her and Worthington both seventh-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Shaped by Silliman at Yale, Müller in Berlin
narrative_blocks.body: His Yale years (graduated 1825) put him under Benjamin Silliman, the chemistry and natural history professor who championed empirical observation as the basis of American science. Later study of the German anatomist Johannes Müller's comparative physiology shaped Hooker's textbooks on Human Physiology and his rationalist defense of evidence-based therapeutics against homeopathy.

narrative_blocks.header: Brothers framed Eli Ives at Yale, 1817-1852
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother George took the same Yale M.D. degree in 1817 and apprenticed directly with Dr. Eli Ives, then Yale's Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine. Thirty-five years later in 1852, Worthington was chosen to succeed Ives in that same Yale chair when Ives retired — making the Hooker brothers the bookends of one of New Haven's longest medical legacies.

education.notes: Medical studies in Philadelphia and Cambridge; Harvard M.D. 1829.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Public elementary and middle school in New Haven CT named after him; original 180 Canner Street building erected 1900; new middle school campus opened 2009.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven, CT — gravestone reads 'Beloved Physician'

career.notes: Practiced medicine in Norwich, Connecticut for 23 years before his Yale appointment. His wife Mary Ingersoll died here in early 1853 just months after the family relocated to New Haven.

career.role: Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine
career.notes: Succeeded Dr. Eli Ives in the chair when Ives resigned. Held the professorship until his death; combined teaching with active medical practice and continued publication.
Elected VP of the AMA in 1864 — among the highest national positions in mid-19th-century American medicine.

notable.notable_blurb: uncle to the medical-textbook author Dr. Worthington Hooker

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, Hampden County MA — returned to family burial ground

education.notes: Yale 1827 — youngest of the four Hooker brothers to attend Yale (John 1810, George 1814, Worthington 1825, Richard 1827).

career.notes: Settled in Macon Georgia after Yale and ordination; ministered there for several decades. His son Thomas was born at Macon in 1849.

narrative_blocks.header: Headed south after Yale, settled in Macon
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale 1827 he was ordained Presbyterian minister and settled in Macon, Bibb County, Georgia — the southernmost outpost of any Hooker brother of his generation. His three older brothers stayed close to Connecticut and Massachusetts in medicine and law; Richard took the gospel south.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Pope of Yale's granddaughter at thirty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: On 15 July 1846 he married Aurelia Dwight at New Haven CT — daughter of James Dwight III and granddaughter of Rev. Timothy Dwight IV, the eighth president of Yale College. The marriage came eighteen years after his Yale graduation and uncle Worthington's later Yale professorship.

narrative_blocks.header: Their son carried two Hooker lines at once
narrative_blocks.body: Their only child Thomas was born at Macon GA in September 1849 — a dual-line Hooker descendant through both parents. From his father Thomas inherited the direct John Hooker descent; from his mother, descent via Mary Hooker's marriage to Rev. James Pierpont in 1690s New Haven, through Sarah Pierpont's marriage to theologian Jonathan Edwards, through Mary Edwards Dwight to Pope of Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-nine in Middletown, buried home
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Middletown, Connecticut on 19 December 1857 at age forty-nine, while north on a visit from Macon. His body was returned to Springfield Cemetery in Hampden County MA to lie with his Hooker parents and Dwight grandparents.

narrative_blocks.header: Posthumously, brother-in-law became Yale's twelfth president
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Aurelia's younger brother Timothy Dwight V was eighteen at the time of Richard's 1846 marriage to her and twenty-nine when Richard died in 1857. Twenty-nine years after Richard's death, Timothy became the twelfth President of Yale in 1886 — the Hooker-Dwight family's second Yale presidency in three generations after Pope Dwight 1795-1817.

career.notes: Practiced medicine at Westhampton MA per EH 1909 #529. Established the Hooker physician tradition in Westhampton continued by son Dr. Anson Hooker (H01414, 1799-1869) and through several Westhampton descendants.
Per EH 1909 #529: 'He was a physician and also a trader at West Hampton, Mass.' — supplementing physician income with a trading operation, common pattern for rural New England doctors of the period.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety-four as Westhampton physician
narrative_blocks.body: Born Northampton MA 1766; died Westhampton MA February 27, 1861 at age ninety-four — extraordinary longevity spanning the early Republic through the Civil War's outbreak. Practiced medicine in Westhampton MA and also operated as a trader; both occupations supported a large family of seven children with first wife Anna Thompson before her 1810 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost wife at forty-two, remarried after two years
narrative_blocks.body: First wife Anna Thompson died Westhampton April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving him with seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. William remarried 1812 to Rhoda Phelps, widow of Ensign Hartland of Hartland Connecticut. No documented children from the second marriage; Rhoda predeceased William by fourteen years.

parents.father_name: Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT (per EH 1909 #529)
parents.father_research_notes: Per Edward Hooker 1909 #529: 'm. 1798 [sic — likely 1788], Anna Thompson, daughter of Hugh Thompson of East Windsor, Conn.' EH date '1798' may be transcription error; her 1786 first child suggests marriage 1785-86. Hugh Thompson East Windsor specific parentage not yet built.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age forty-two leaving seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Died Westhampton MA April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. Her son middle-named for her father Hugh Thompson (Hugh Thompson Hooker, born 1789) carries the East Windsor Connecticut Thompson family name forward.

bio.extra_names: Widow of Ensign Hartland when she married Dr. William Hooker 1812

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Westhampton MA — alongside husband Dr. William Hooker.

parents.father_research_notes: Phelps family of Hartland CT. Specific parents not identified.

military_service.war: Likely War of 1812 (age 26 at outbreak; rank Capt confirmed)
military_service.notes: Findagrave Memorial #122605184 carries 'VETERAN' marker. Captain rank confirmed in displayed name. Born 1786 — would have been 26 at outbreak of War of 1812. Specific unit + service dates not yet documented.

narrative_blocks.header: Married at seventeen, first child at eighteen
narrative_blocks.body: Married Sylvia Wales 1803 in Westhampton when both were seventeen years old. Their first child Emily Hooker Kingsley was born May 1804 when Henry was eighteen. Five children followed over seventeen years, the last (Almira Hooker, born 1820) carrying the name of Henry's young-deceased sister Almira Hooker Hale who had died age twenty-four in 1816.

parents.father_research_notes: Dr. William Hooker (H00529, 1766-?), physician + trader at West Hampton MA; born Northampton MA Nov 26, 1766; son of Rev John and Sarah Worthington Hooker of Northampton. Per EH 1909 #529: 'William Hooker (John* John* John,3 Samuel,2 Thomas1)...m. 1798 Anna Thompson, daughter of Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT. She died at West Hampton MA. He married No. 2, Widow of Ensign Hartland.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Thompson Hooker not yet in tree.

career.location: West Hampton, Norwich, and Westfield Massachusetts
career.notes: Merchant at West Hampton MA, then Norwich MA. In 1819 removed family to Westfield MA, where they remained.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved family from Norwich Massachusetts to Westfield, 1819
narrative_blocks.body: Merchant at West Hampton MA before relocating to Norwich MA where his two eldest children Lucy Ashmun and William Throop were born. In 1819 he moved the family to Westfield MA, where son Edward was born 1822 and where William and his wife Octavia would both be buried at Pine Hill Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Lucy Ashmun died at Rushville Illinois, 1839
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest child Lucy Ashmun died age 26 at Rushville IL April 17, 1839 — only seventeen months after her Massachusetts marriage to Dr Joseph Washburn Clark and five weeks after the birth of their infant son who himself died in May. The Illinois frontier had taken his daughter, son-in-law's first family, and grandson all within a year.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #1411: 'He was a trader and farmer at West Hampton, Mass.'

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Thompson honors maternal grandfather Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT, father of Anna Thompson Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Children died at twenty and twenty-two; outlived both
narrative_blocks.body: Worthington died August 1, 1843 at age twenty, and his sister Sarah Judd died February 6, 1848 at age twenty-two — both before their father, within five years of each other. Hugh Thompson outlived them both by a decade, dying at Westhampton April 20, 1856; his wife Sarah Judd Hooker followed seven years later.

bio.bio_blurb: Hooker daughter who married Dr. Enoch Hale

burial.plot_notes: Gravestone inscription: 'Almira, only Daughter of Dctr. William and Mrs. Anna Hooker, was born at Westhampton Nov. 10th 1792. She was married to Doctr. Enoch Hale Sept. 6th 1813 and died at Gardiner Dec. 5th 1816.'

cross_connections.display_label: First cousin Sarah Hooker became Enoch's second wife, 1822

narrative_blocks.header: Dead at twenty-three; niece bore her name
narrative_blocks.body: In September 1813, Almira married Enoch Hale, a physician and son of West Hampton's first minister — the same year her brother William had married Enoch's sister Octavia, binding two Hooker children and two Hale siblings in the same Westhampton community. Almira died at Gardiner Maine on December 5, 1816 at age twenty-four, only three years into the marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Hookers married two Hales
narrative_blocks.body: In neighboring years a Hooker brother and sister each married a Hale: her brother William wed Octavia Hale in 1811, and she married Octavia’s brother, Dr. Enoch Hale, in 1813 — a double knot between two Westhampton families.

marriages.notes: EH records unnamed infant (b. and d. 1832) by this marriage — non-standard EH ID '2760a'; no JSON entry created pending compiler decision on alphanumeric IDs.

career.notes: Per EH 1909: 'not only became noted as a physician but was also prominent in the public affairs of the place.'
Representative of East Cambridge in the Massachusetts General Court per EH 1909.

cross_connections.display_label: Studied medicine at East Cambridge; died of hospital disease, 1858
Son who joined his practice; died New Year's Eve 1873

narrative_blocks.header: Nephew died six weeks after son's birth, 1858
narrative_blocks.body: His nephew Edward Hooker came to East Cambridge to study medicine after graduating Williams College in 1855, but died April 26, 1858 of disease contracted in hospital service — six weeks after Anson's youngest son Edward Dwight was born in the same East Cambridge household on March 17. The same spring brought both a birth and a death into the Hooker physician line.

narrative_blocks.header: Common Council to State House in one career
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond his East Cambridge medical practice, Anson served successively on the Common Council, as Alderman, and as a Representative in the Massachusetts General Court — three ascending levels of civic office held while maintaining his practice from 1822 until his death in 1869. Harvard-trained physicians of his generation were expected to lead their communities as well as treat them.

narrative_blocks.header: First of three consecutive Harvard physician generations, 1822
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Anson Hooker graduated Harvard Medical School in 1822 and built a Cambridge practice that his son Anson Parker inherited after Harvard M.D. 1855, and his grandson Edward Dwight carried on after Harvard M.D. 1884. Three consecutive Hooker physicians trained at Harvard across sixty-two years.

career.notes: First law practice after Williams College 1820.
Returned to Massachusetts; practiced law at South Hadley. Met and married Mary Ann Dwight here 1834.

Per obituary: 'lawyery at D.H. Lamb & Co.' EH 1909 says 'engaged in soda manufacture' — D.H. Lamb & Co. may have been the soda manufacturing enterprise for which Edward served as legal counsel and commercial partner. Removed from Rochester NY to Cleveland 1835; remained until his death August 1, 1841 at thirty-nine.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-nine; wife outlived him fifty years
narrative_blocks.body: Edward left West Hampton MA as a Williams College graduate, practiced law in Ohio and Massachusetts, then pivoted to soda manufacturing in Cleveland — dying there August 1, 1841 at only thirty-nine, leaving two children under age seven. His wife Mary Ann Dwight survived him until June 4, 1891, outliving him by exactly fifty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Became, late in life, a man of prayer
narrative_blocks.body: The obituary that marked Edward's death at thirty-nine focused less on his law practice at D.H. Lamb & Co. than on the man he had become: late in life he united with the Congregational Church in Ohio City and was remembered by those who knew him simply as 'a man of prayer.' For a direct descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker — who helped establish the Congregational tradition in Connecticut two centuries earlier — the phrase carried the full weight of inheritance.

narrative_blocks.header: Williams 1820; youngest son of a man who
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker graduated Williams College in 1820 and practiced law at Ravenna Ohio, then South Hadley Massachusetts, before settling in Cleveland with D.H. Lamb & Co. He was the youngest son of Dr. William Hooker of Westhampton, who lived to ninety-four — Edward died at thirty-nine.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #530: 'an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vt.'

narrative_blocks.header: Apothecary and grocer at Rutland; wife predeceased him
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker was an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vermont, where he spent his adult life and died in 1836 at sixty-five. His wife Sarah Brown died four years before him in 1832, predeceasing him despite being nine years younger.

narrative_blocks.header: Married daughter of Col. John Brown of Pittsfield
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Brown's father, Colonel John Brown of Pittsfield, Massachusetts, is almost certainly the Revolutionary War officer killed at the Battle of Stone Arabia, New York, on October 19, 1780 — when Sarah was barely a year old. If confirmed, Thomas Hooker married the daughter of a Revolutionary War martyr.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin sons: one to Boston missions
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington and Henry Brown Hooker were born the same day — August 31, 1802 — and their lives diverged completely: John moved to Chicago in 1836 among the city's earliest New England settlers, while Henry graduated Middlebury College, entered the ministry, and became Secretary of Missions at Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: Two of five children died in Chicago
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington Hooker moved to Chicago in 1836 and died there in 1866; his sister Julia Ann married a Rutland lawyer and also died in Chicago in 1842. Two of Thomas Hooker's five children ended their days in the city that barely existed when they were born.

narrative_blocks.header: Probably daughter of a Revolutionary War martyr
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Brown's father, Colonel John Brown of Pittsfield Massachusetts named in the Hooker genealogy, is almost certainly the Revolutionary War officer killed at the Battle of Stone Arabia, New York, on October 19, 1780 — when Sarah was barely a year old. She married Thomas Hooker in 1796 and died at Rutland, Vermont, in 1832, four years before her husband.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Congregational minister; moved Vermont to Wisconsin
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth White Hooker married Rev. Josiah F. Goodhue in 1824 — a Middlebury College and Andover Theological Seminary graduate who served Congregational churches at Williston and Shoreham, Vermont, for twenty-four years. In 1857 they removed to Whitewater, Wisconsin, where he died in 1863 and she in January 1889 at eighty-eight.

career.notes: Merchant at Rutland VT per EH.
Moved to Chicago 1836 per EH. Died there 1866.

cross_connections.display_label: His twin's daughter became a missionary in South India, 1857

narrative_blocks.header: Settled Chicago in 1836; city three years old
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington Hooker left Rutland Vermont in 1836 — the same year his father Thomas died — and moved to Chicago, then just three years old as an incorporated city, growing rapidly from a frontier trading post. He died there in 1866, having watched Chicago transform from a village of hundreds into a city approaching three hundred thousand.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin of Henry
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington and his twin Henry Brown Hooker were born on August 31, 1802 — the same morning — and their lives diverged entirely: John to Chicago merchant life on the frontier, Henry to Congregational ministry and Boston missions. Their mother Sarah Brown died when they were twenty-nine.

narrative_blocks.header: No children recorded
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington Hooker moved to Chicago in 1836 — the year his father Thomas died in Rutland — and spent thirty years as a merchant there until his own death in 1866. No children are documented in the Hooker genealogy, and he left no recorded descendants.

career.notes: One year as home missionary in South Carolina after ordination October 10, 1825.
May 2, 1827 to May 17, 1836 — about 10 years.
February 1837 to June 1858 — about 21 years. Obituary says 'about 10 years' — likely referring to active full-time pastorate.
Secretary 1858-1873; succeeded Rev. Dr. Clark. Continued assisting the society until death July 4, 1881.
Member Massachusetts Board of Education 1844-1851.

education.notes: Graduated Middlebury College 1821. Obituary source: Middlebury Register July 8, 1881.

education.institution_blurb: Graduated Andover Theological Seminary 1825; ordained October 10, 1825.

institutions.institution_blurb: Corporate member from 1845; daughter Sarah was ABCFM missionary in India.
American Tract Society published eight tracts from Rev. Hooker's pen.
Secretary of Massachusetts Home Missionary Society 1858-1873.

narrative_blocks.header: Secretary of Massachusetts Home Missionary Society, 1858-1873
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Henry Brown Hooker served as secretary of the Massachusetts Home Missionary Society from 1858 to 1873, succeeding Rev. Dr. Clark — capping a ministry that had included pastorates in South Carolina, Lanesboro, and Falmouth, Massachusetts. He continued assisting the society until his death on July 4, 1881, Independence Day, in Boston at seventy-eight.

narrative_blocks.header: American Tract Society published eight of his tracts
narrative_blocks.body: The American Tract Society published eight tracts from Rev. Hooker's pen, and he wrote three more for the Tract Society of Boston, as well as two Sunday-school books — 'Plea for the Heathen' and 'Put Off and Put On' — that had large sales. His obituary noted that his contributions to the religious press were 'frequent and popular.'

narrative_blocks.header: Twin who chose ministry while his brother John
narrative_blocks.body: Born the same day as his twin John Worthington Hooker on August 31, 1802, Henry took the path of Congregational ministry while John moved to Chicago's frontier in 1836. Henry's daughter Sarah Brown Hooker would carry the evangelical tradition a generation further — thirty years as a missionary to South India.

narrative_blocks.header: ABCFM corporate member from 1845
narrative_blocks.body: From 1845, Rev. Hooker served as a corporate member of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions — the same organization his son-in-law Rev. William Banfield Capron would serve as a missionary in South India from 1857. His daughter Sarah spent thirty years at the ABCFM mission in Madura, a direct extension of her father's decades of institutional commitment.

narrative_blocks.header: Born 1806; died 1830 at twenty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Strong Hooker was born in 1806 at Rutland, the fourth of five children, and died in 1830 at about twenty-four — surviving her twin brothers by childhood and predeceasing her younger sister Julia. No spouse or children are recorded in the Hooker genealogy.

narrative_blocks.header: Married the Vermont Adjutant General; died in Chicago
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Ann Hooker married Frederick William Hopkins — who served as Adjutant and Inspector General of Vermont and compiled the state's military laws — in December 1836, the same year her brother John moved to Chicago. She died in Chicago September 27, 1842, one of two Thomas Hooker children to die in that city.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-three; daughter Sarah Hooker survived her
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Ann died in Chicago at about thirty-three in 1842, leaving a daughter Sarah Hooker Hopkins — whose middle name Hooker honored the family line — who was just five years old. Her husband Frederick survived her and continued his Vermont legal career.

burial.plot_notes: West Street Cemetery, Rutland VT. FGA #. She died in Chicago but was buried in the family cemetery in Rutland.

narrative_blocks.header: Amiableness of the friend, gentleness of the mother
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1812 Northampton obituary described Lucy Hooker Ashmun as a woman of uncommon merits: 'the amiableness of the friend, the cheerful, mild disposition of the wife, the gentleness of the mother.' She died after a long illness at thirty-seven, leaving five children — the eldest just twelve.

narrative_blocks.header: Died four years before Eli reached the Senate
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy Hooker Ashmun died in 1812, four years before her husband Eli Porter Ashmun was elected to the United States Senate — she never saw the political career that would make his name. Their son George and son John Hooker Ashmun would go on to federal office and Harvard Law, carrying the legacy she did not live to witness.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Northampton's Rev. John Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy was the daughter of Rev. John Hooker of Northampton, Massachusetts — a great-granddaughter in the line descending from Rev. Thomas Hooker who founded Hartford in 1636. She married into the Ashmun family in 1798, and her middle name Hooker would be carried by her daughter and then her granddaughter across two more generations.

I00168.prominent_name_reason: Eli Porter Ashmun (1770-1819) — US Senator from Massachusetts 1816-1818 (Federalist). Filled vacancy caused by Christopher Gore's resignation. Founded first law practice in Blandford MA. Mentored by Theodore Sedgwick. Father of George Ashmun (US Congressman, presided over 1860 Republican convention that nominated Abraham Lincoln) and John Hooker Ashmun (Harvard Law professor, first buried on Harvard Hill).

political_offices.notes: Filled vacancy caused by Christopher Gore's resignation.

cross_connections.display_label: His grandson Lewis Jr. reached California; died 1930

narrative_blocks.header: Filled Christopher Gore's Senate seat in 1816
narrative_blocks.body: Eli Porter Ashmun was elected to the United States Senate in 1816 to fill the vacancy left by Christopher Gore's resignation, serving until 1818 — the first of two generations of Ashmun men in federal office. His son George would preside over the 1860 Republican National Convention that nominated Abraham Lincoln, placing a Hooker-descendant family at the center of American politics across two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Family fled Burgoyne's advance as a child, 1777
narrative_blocks.body: The Ashmun family fled their Fort Edward New York home in 1777 when British General John Burgoyne's forces advanced through the Champlain Valley during the Saratoga campaign — Eli was seven years old. They settled in Blandford Massachusetts, where after schooling described as 'quite rudimentary' he was taken under wing by Congressman Theodore Sedgwick, studied law, and opened the first legal practice in town.

narrative_blocks.header: State rep, state senator, governor's council, then Senate
narrative_blocks.body: Eli Porter Ashmun climbed Massachusetts's political ladder methodically — state representative 1803-1804, state senator 1808-1810, member of the Governor's Council in 1816, then United States Senator the same year. His rise from tavern-keeper's son with rudimentary schooling to United States Senator was the full arc of early American democratic possibility.

narrative_blocks.header: Son George chaired Lincoln's 1860 nomination convention
narrative_blocks.body: George Ashmun, born to Eli and Lucy Hooker Ashmun in 1804, served four terms in the United States Congress and then presided as chairman of the 1860 Republican National Convention that nominated Abraham Lincoln for president. Lincoln personally wrote George a note on the final day of his life — among the last things the President ever wrote.

education.notes: Honorary degree — not a regular graduation. Wikipedia and Congressional bio both confirm.

burial.plot_notes: Bridge Street Cemetery, Northampton MA. Buried next to Eli, Lucy, and Orpha.

narrative_blocks.header: Died one year after her senator father, 1820
narrative_blocks.body: Sophia W. Ashmun died in 1820, one year after her father Eli Porter Ashmun died in 1819 — two deaths in rapid succession that left the four surviving Ashmun children without both parents by the time Sophia was eighteen. Her brother George, who was fifteen at her death, would go on to preside over Abraham Lincoln's 1860 presidential nomination.

HD0610.prominent_name_reason: John Hooker Ashmun (1800-1833) — Royal Professor of Law, Harvard. Named "Hooker" for Hooker ancestry. First person buried on Harvard Hill (Harvard-owned lot at Mount Auburn Cemetery). Died age 32 of slow disease; his gravestone inscription written by his pupils.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Auburn Cemetery, Cambridge MA. First person buried on Harvard Hill (Harvard-owned lot).

sources.short: Find A Grave, Bridge Street Cemetery record

bio.notes: Named "John Hooker" honoring maternal grandfather Rev. John Hooker (H00264)

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard Law professor; died at thirty-three
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker Ashmun joined the Harvard Law faculty and was regarded as one of the most promising legal scholars of his generation before dying at thirty-three in 1833. His middle name Hooker honored the Ashmun connection to the Hooker family through his mother Lucy — a naming pattern his younger sister Lucy would carry as well.

narrative_blocks.header: Ran Northampton Law School; joined Harvard before thirty
narrative_blocks.body: Before joining the Harvard Law faculty, John Hooker Ashmun operated the Northampton Law School for several years — training lawyers in the same Massachusetts legal world his father Eli Porter Ashmun had helped build. He joined Harvard before thirty and died in office at thirty-three, his reputation outlasting his brief career.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker after his mother; sister followed suit
narrative_blocks.body: John's middle name Hooker honored the Ashmun family's connection to the Hooker line through his mother Lucy Hooker Ashmun. His sister Lucy shared the same middle name for the same reason, and a generation later his niece Lucy Hooker Ashmun Hedden would carry it into a third generation.

bio.notes: Named "Lucy Hooker" honoring Hooker maternal line

naming_inspiration.description: Named Lucy Hooker after her mother Lucy Hooker Ashmun — the second of three Ashmun women to carry this name (mother H00532, herself, then niece HD4946 Lucy Hooker Ashmun Hedden).

narrative_blocks.header: Named for her mother; both died young
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy Hooker Ashmun carried the same name as her mother, who had died in 1812 when Lucy was just five years old. She herself died in 1836 at twenty-nine, the second Lucy Hooker in the Ashmun family to die young — and her niece would become the third to carry the name.

burial.plot_notes: Green River Cemetery, Greenfield, Franklin County, Massachusetts. FGA #136306610.

career.notes: Died aboard sloop of war USS Decatur off Nicaragua, March 2, 1852.

narrative_blocks.header: Died aboard the Decatur off Nicaragua, 1852
narrative_blocks.body: Lewis Ashmun died March 2, 1852 aboard the USS Decatur — a sloop of war — off Nicaragua, the youngest of Senator Eli Porter Ashmun's five children, at forty-three. Nicaragua in 1852 was a zone of intense American naval interest, where the Vanderbilt-backed transit route made it the fastest path to the California gold fields.

narrative_blocks.header: Son Lewis Jr. outlived him by seventy-eight years
narrative_blocks.body: Lewis Ashmun died at sea in 1852 at forty-three, leaving a five-year-old son who would outlive him by seventy-eight years, dying in Los Angeles in 1930. That son carried the senator's line west across a continent his grandfather Eli Porter Ashmun — a Fort Edward refugee who rose to the United States Senate — could never have imagined.

narrative_blocks.header: Younger brother to a senator's son
narrative_blocks.body: Lewis Ashmun was the youngest of Senator Eli Porter Ashmun's five children — younger brother to Congressman George Ashmun, who would preside over Lincoln's 1860 nomination, and to John Hooker Ashmun, professor at Harvard Law. He died a mariner off Nicaragua while his brothers were among the most distinguished Hooker descendants of their generation.

HD0613.prominent_name_reason: George Ashmun (1804-1870) — Whig/Republican US Congressman from Massachusetts, presided over the 1860 Republican National Convention that nominated Abraham Lincoln. Yale 1823. Co-founder of the Republican Party with Samuel Bowles. Son of US Senator Eli Porter Ashmun and Lucy Hooker (H00532), great-great-great-grandson of Thomas Hooker. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Ashmun

cross_connections.display_label: Mark Howard (b.1817 Kent, England; d.1887 West Hartford CT) —.
Both Republican Party founders (Ashmun MA chair 1860; Anthony IL)
Nephew Lewis Jr. carried the family to California, 1930

cross_connections.notes: Ashmun presided over the 1860 Republican National Convention that nominated Lincoln. Foote was a speaker at the 1856 Republican Party organizing meeting in New Haven CT. Two Hooker-line connections to the founding of the Republican Party.

narrative_blocks.header: Chaired the 1860 convention that nominated Lincoln
narrative_blocks.body: George Ashmun presided as chairman of the 1860 Republican National Convention in Chicago — the gathering that made Abraham Lincoln the Republican nominee for president. On April 14, 1865, the final day of Lincoln's life, the President personally wrote Ashmun a note admitting him to the White House: it is among the last things Lincoln ever wrote.

narrative_blocks.header: Father a senator; son served four congressional terms
narrative_blocks.body: His father Eli Porter Ashmun served in the United States Senate from 1816 to 1818; George followed into federal office with four terms in the Massachusetts House delegation from 1845 to 1851. He retired from Congress fourteen years before presiding at the convention that made Lincoln's presidency possible.

narrative_blocks.header: Lincoln wrote to him hours before the assassination
narrative_blocks.body: On April 14, 1865, Abraham Lincoln wrote George Ashmun a personal note admitting him to the White House: 'Allow Mr. Ashmun & friend to come in. —A Lincoln.' Lincoln was shot at Ford's Theatre that same evening; the note is among the last things the President ever wrote, and is preserved in presidential archives.
